blog.title: A positive idea/event for rainbow community in recently in NZ
blog.body:
Let's talk about the snail.

Not the fastest creature, the snail. Not the flashiest. But here's the thing: a snail carries its whole home on its back, wherever it goes. Big city, tiny town, the middle of your nan's garden — home comes too. Which is exactly why InsideOUT Kōaro chose a snail as the mascot for Schools' Pride Week Aotearoa 2026, held 15–19 June, with the theme "Community is Home."

The idea is that takatāpui and rainbow people carry home, identity and community with them everywhere — from the biggest cities right down to the smallest regional towns. And honestly? For those of us in Te Tau Ihu, the top of the South, that lands. You don't have to move to Auckland or Wellington to belong. Your people are already here. Slow, steady, shimmering — the snail gets it.

Here's the part that made us grin. Over 250 schools and community groups took part in 2026 (Scoop News, 19 June 2026) — early childhood centres, kura, primary, intermediate, secondary, tertiary providers and community orgs, loads of them for the very first time. That number matters because it's a comeback. Participation had dipped over the previous couple of years, worn down by a lot of anti-rainbow noise online. So more schools showing up this year isn't a small thing. It's communities choosing aroha out loud, on purpose, after a hard stretch. That's hope you can actually stand on.

Why does it matter so much that a school throws a bit of a party for its rainbow rangatahi? Because feeling safe at school changes everything. As Judy O'Brien, Chief Executive of InsideOUT Kōaro, put it: "Feeling safe, celebrated and valued at school is foundational to young people's wellbeing and learning." The flip side is real too — around 41% of rainbow students said they'd been bullied at school in the past year, compared with 23% of their classmates (Scoop News, 19 June 2026). So when 250-plus schools decide to celebrate openly, they're not just decorating a hallway. They're saying: you're safe here, and we're glad you're you.

Schools' Pride Week is nationwide, which means schools across the motu — including here in the top of the South — were part of this wave. There was also a lovely letter-writing project, where rainbow adults and young people wrote affirmations, hopes and dreams for takatāpui, MVPFAFF+ and rainbow rangatahi. Chosen whānau and blood whānau both make a home, and this was whānau in ink.

Want to know more, or get your school involved next time? Have a kōrero with InsideOUT Kōaro (insideout.org.nz) or the Schools' Pride Week hub (pride.school.nz). And if you're after something local and coming up: Nelson Provincial Museum is running "OUR VISION: Rangatahi, Imagining the Future, Shaping Tomorrow," a Nelson Youth Council exhibition, from 11 September to 18 October 2026 — free for Nelson Tasman locals. Not rainbow-specific, but a genuine celebration of young people shaping tomorrow, right on our doorstep.

So here's the takeaway, snail-style: home isn't a postcode. It's the people who make you feel safe being unapologetically yourself — and 250-plus schools just proved that community is already here, growing, one steady trail at a time. You belong here.

If you ever need someone to talk to, we've got you. Reach out through the Q Youth contact page at qyouthnz.com, or call OUTLine NZ on 0800 688 5463 or Youthline on 0800 376 633. Any time, any day.

blog.title: Welcome to the Q Youth blog
blog.body:
Kia ora! This is our new blog — a space where we'll share news, updates, and kōrero on topical issues that matter to rainbow rangatahi and their whānau in Te Tau Ihu.

Check back here for posts about what's happening at Q Youth, upcoming events, and issues affecting our community. If there's a topic you'd like us to cover, get in touch — we'd love to hear from you.
